([Bellino](/wiki/Bellino "Bellino"), 14 July 1881 – [Chiavari](/wiki/Chiavari "Chiavari"), 3 July 1952) was an Italian general during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Charles D. Pettibone, The Organization and Order of Battle of Militaries in World War II Volume VI Italy and France Including the Neutral Countries of San Marino, Vatican City (Holy See), Andorra, and Monaco, pp. 21-74-104

**Matteo Roux** ([Bellino](/wiki/Bellino "Bellino"), 14 July 1881 – [Chiavari](/wiki/Chiavari "Chiavari"), 3 July 1952) was an Italian general during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Matteo_Roux&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

The second of three children, he was born in Bellino, [province of Cuneo](/wiki/Province_of_Cuneo "Province of Cuneo"), on 14 July 1881, the son of Bernardo Roux, a [craftsman](/wiki/Artisan "Artisan") by profession, and of Maria Allemand. At a very young age he emigrated with his family to [Toulon](/wiki/Toulon "Toulon"), France, when his father made a fortune by selling a new type of [liqueur](/wiki/Liqueur "Liqueur"), until he decided to return to Italy and settle in [Cuneo](/wiki/Cuneo "Cuneo"), where he started a new business as a restorer. The young Matteo became passionate about the world of [horse riding](/wiki/Horse_riding "Horse riding"), and eventually decided to enroll in the Cavalry School, thus starting his military career. He enlisted in the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") in 1900 and began to attend the Royal Military Academy of Artillery and Engineers in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), graduating with the rank of artillery [second lieutenant](/wiki/Second_lieutenant "Second lieutenant") on 13 November 1901. Having become a brilliant horse riding instructor, he was called by the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") to train [Crown Prince Umberto](/wiki/Umberto_II_of_Italy "Umberto II of Italy") and later [Prince Amedeo of Savoy-Aosta](/wiki/Prince_Amedeo,_Duke_of_Aosta "Prince Amedeo, Duke of Aosta"). He thus gained access to the royal palaces, learning how to move in such environments with ease, and met Margherita Beauty, a young lady belonging to the nobility of a younger branch of the Savoy family, whom he married in 1903.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

He later attended the War School of Turin and became an officer of the General Staff, participating in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). Between 1920 and 1928 he served at the War School of Turin (initially as chief instructor of [military history](/wiki/Military_history "Military history"), then as instructor of [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics") and finally as chief instructor of logistics), being promoted to [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel") and later to [brigadier general](/wiki/Brigadier_general "Brigadier general") on 16 June 1934. Between 1935 and 1936 he took part in the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") and in the subsequent [counterguerrilla](/wiki/Counterguerrilla "Counterguerrilla") operations. After being repatriated he was promoted to [major general](/wiki/Major_general "Major general") on 1 July 1937, and assigned to the command of the artillery in [Brescia](/wiki/Brescia "Brescia"). He then assumed the post of Chief of Staff of the [V Corps](/wiki/V_Army_Corps_(Italy) "V Army Corps (Italy)") of [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"). After serving as Director General of Logistics Services at the Ministry of War, in 1939 he assumed command of the [3rd Infantry Division "Monferrato"](/wiki/3rd_Infantry_Division_Ravenna "3rd Infantry Division Ravenna") (renamed 3rd Infantry Division "Ravenna" in March 1939), and immediately after the [occupation of Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") he was appointed commander of the troops deployed there, being later replaced by [Carlo Geloso](/wiki/Carlo_Geloso "Carlo Geloso"). After promotion to [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general") on 1 January 1940, he assumed command of the [XI Army Corps](/wiki/XI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XI Army Corps (Italy)"), which he maintained after the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") entered the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") on 10 June 1940.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

With the beginning of the [Greek campaign](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War"), due to the unfavorable trend of the military operations, on 1 November 1940 he was replaced by General [Mario Robotti](/wiki/Mario_Robotti "Mario Robotti"), whom in turn he replaced as director of the logistic services of the Ministry of War in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). Within a few days he lost both his wife and his daughter Maria, who died after contracting [influenza](/wiki/Influenza "Influenza") while they were awaiting his return in [Bari](/wiki/Bari "Bari"). In the following years he worked in Rome as Director of Logistic Services at the Ministry of War and continued to frequent the House of Savoy. Having fallen in love with his young [maid](/wiki/Maid "Maid") Maria Manzo, who gave him a son, Mario, he married her a few years later. During this period he built a villa in Fontanile, a hamlet of the municipality of Bellino.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

After being discharged from the Army he retired to private life, living between Bellino and [Saluzzo](/wiki/Saluzzo "Saluzzo") for several years. On 1 June 1949 the [Prefecture](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture") of Cuneo appointed him President of the local hospital. He died suddenly on 3 July 1952, while visiting his sister Margherita in [Chiavari](/wiki/Chiavari "Chiavari").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)
